title: Early Adopters & Customer Development

introduction.subtitle: Knowledge quiz. 8-10 min.
introduction.description:
Early Adopters & Customer Development is an educational challenge for Adopter Segmentation, Chasm & Whole Product, Customer Discovery, Customer Validation. It teaches through plausible wrong answers and post-result explanations.

Choose the answer you would use in a real founder or product conversation. Your result stays in this browser unless you explicitly share it.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is explicit and share-safe.
introduction.disclaimer: Educational diagnostic only. Not legal, tax, financial, investment, medical, employment, regulatory, or psychometric certification advice.

questions.text: Which description best identifies an early adopter?
questions.explanation: Early adopters usually have urgent pain, seek alternatives, tolerate incompleteness, and can influence the next reference group.

options.text: A. Anyone who likes new technology.
B. A customer with urgent pain, a current workaround, and willingness to try an incomplete solution.
C. The largest mainstream buyer in the market.
D. A user who never pays but enjoys demos.

conceptHelp.title: Adopter Segmentation: Which description best identifies an early adopter
conceptHelp.summary: Adopter Segmentation is tested here through a concrete operating question: "Which description best identifies an early adopter?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The strongest answer identifies the mechanism that would still hold up in a real founder, product, or investor conversation.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by mechanism, not by familiar vocabulary. Pick the answer you would defend in a real decision conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "A customer with urgent pain, a current workaround, and willingness to try an incomplete solution."

questions.text: What is the main risk of confusing visionaries with pragmatists?
questions.explanation: Visionaries may accept novelty and custom work, while pragmatists often need references, risk reduction, and a complete solution.

options.text: A. The product will become too cheap.
B. The team may mistake enthusiasm for repeatable mainstream demand.
C. The team will interview too many customers.
D. The market category will disappear automatically.

conceptHelp.title: Adopter Segmentation: What is the main risk of confusing visionaries with pragmatists
conceptHelp.summary: Adopter Segmentation is tested here through a concrete operating question: "What is the main risk of confusing visionaries with pragmatists?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The strongest answer identifies the mechanism that would still hold up in a real founder, product, or investor conversation.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by mechanism, not by familiar vocabulary. Pick the answer you would defend in a real decision conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "The team may mistake enthusiasm for repeatable mainstream demand."

questions.text: Which signal best suggests a beachhead segment is worth focus?
questions.explanation: A strong beachhead has repeated pain, reachable buyers, similar use cases, and references that matter inside the segment.

options.text: A. Many unrelated users say the product is interesting.
B. One broad analyst report names the market.
C. A narrow segment shares urgent pain, similar workflow, reachable buyers, and credible reference potential.
D. The segment is fashionable this month.

conceptHelp.title: Adopter Segmentation: Which signal best suggests a beachhead segment is worth focus
conceptHelp.summary: Adopter Segmentation is tested here through a concrete operating question: "Which signal best suggests a beachhead segment is worth focus?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The strongest answer identifies the mechanism that would still hold up in a real founder, product, or investor conversation.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by mechanism, not by familiar vocabulary. Pick the answer you would defend in a real decision conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "A narrow segment shares urgent pain, similar workflow, reachable buyers, and credible reference potential."

questions.text: What does the chasm represent in early-market strategy?
questions.explanation: The chasm is the difficult transition from visionary early adopters to pragmatic early majority buyers.

options.text: A. A gap between early adopters and the pragmatic early majority.
B. The time between two engineering releases.
C. A legal barrier to selling software.
D. The distance between two pricing tiers.

conceptHelp.title: Chasm & Whole Product: What does the chasm represent in early-market strategy
conceptHelp.summary: Chasm & Whole Product is tested here through a concrete operating question: "What does the chasm represent in early-market strategy?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The strongest answer identifies the mechanism that would still hold up in a real founder, product, or investor conversation.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by mechanism, not by familiar vocabulary. Pick the answer you would defend in a real decision conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "A gap between early adopters and the pragmatic early majority."

questions.text: Why does whole-product thinking matter for pragmatist buyers?
questions.explanation: Pragmatists want the complete solution needed to solve the business problem, not just a promising core technology.

options.text: A. They only buy products with the most features.
B. They need the surrounding services, proof, integrations, support, and risk reduction that make adoption safe.
C. They prefer custom demos over references.
D. They ignore switching cost.

conceptHelp.title: Chasm & Whole Product: Why does whole-product thinking matter for pragmatist buyers
conceptHelp.summary: Chasm & Whole Product is tested here through a concrete operating question: "Why does whole-product thinking matter for pragmatist buyers?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The strongest answer identifies the mechanism that would still hold up in a real founder, product, or investor conversation.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by mechanism, not by familiar vocabulary. Pick the answer you would defend in a real decision conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "They need the surrounding services, proof, integrations, support, and risk reduction that make adoption safe."

questions.text: Which go-to-market move best fits chasm strategy?
questions.explanation: Chasm strategy favours a focused beachhead where the company can win references before expanding.

options.text: A. Target every segment at once to avoid missing opportunities.
B. Win one narrow beachhead with a complete solution and credible references.
C. Delay selling until the product has every possible feature.
D. Avoid partners because they complicate the story.

conceptHelp.title: Chasm & Whole Product: Which go-to-market move best fits chasm strategy
conceptHelp.summary: Chasm & Whole Product is tested here through a concrete operating question: "Which go-to-market move best fits chasm strategy?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The strongest answer identifies the mechanism that would still hold up in a real founder, product, or investor conversation.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by mechanism, not by familiar vocabulary. Pick the answer you would defend in a real decision conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "Win one narrow beachhead with a complete solution and credible references."

questions.text: What is Customer Discovery primarily trying to learn?
questions.explanation: Customer Discovery tests whether the problem, customer, and buying context are real before scaling execution.

options.text: A. Whether the founder can write a long roadmap.
B. Whether the problem, customer, workflow, alternatives, and buying trigger are real.
C. Whether the logo design is memorable.
D. Whether every feature can be built this quarter.

conceptHelp.title: Customer Discovery: What is Customer Discovery primarily trying to learn
conceptHelp.summary: Customer Discovery is tested here through a concrete operating question: "What is Customer Discovery primarily trying to learn?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The strongest answer identifies the mechanism that would still hold up in a real founder, product, or investor conversation.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by mechanism, not by familiar vocabulary. Pick the answer you would defend in a real decision conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "Whether the problem, customer, workflow, alternatives, and buying trigger are real."

questions.text: Which interview question is strongest during discovery?
questions.explanation: Behavioural questions about recent real situations produce better evidence than opinions about hypothetical products.

options.text: A. Would you use this if we built it?
B. How much do you like our idea?
C. Tell me about the last time this problem happened and what you did.
D. Which colour should the dashboard use?

conceptHelp.title: Customer Discovery: Which interview question is strongest during discovery
conceptHelp.summary: Customer Discovery is tested here through a concrete operating question: "Which interview question is strongest during discovery?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The strongest answer identifies the mechanism that would still hold up in a real founder, product, or investor conversation.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by mechanism, not by familiar vocabulary. Pick the answer you would defend in a real decision conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "Tell me about the last time this problem happened and what you did."

questions.text: When discovery contradicts the original idea, what should happen?
questions.explanation: Contradictory discovery evidence should update segmentation, problem framing, or solution assumptions.

options.text: A. Ignore it until after launch.
B. Update the hypothesis and test the revised customer, problem, or solution assumption.
C. Increase the advertising budget.
D. Stop talking to customers entirely.

conceptHelp.title: Customer Discovery: When discovery contradicts the original idea, what should happen
conceptHelp.summary: Customer Discovery is tested here through a concrete operating question: "When discovery contradicts the original idea, what should happen?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The strongest answer identifies the mechanism that would still hold up in a real founder, product, or investor conversation.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by mechanism, not by familiar vocabulary. Pick the answer you would defend in a real decision conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "Update the hypothesis and test the revised customer, problem, or solution assumption."

questions.explanation: Customer Validation tests whether a repeatable sales process and business model can work with real customers.

options.text: A. Whether the company has enough social media followers.
B. Whether a repeatable sales process and business model can be validated.
C. Whether every customer wants a custom roadmap.
D. Whether the team can avoid pricing conversations.

conceptHelp.title: Customer Validation: What does Customer Validation test
conceptHelp.summary: Customer Validation is tested here through a concrete operating question: "What does Customer Validation test?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The strongest answer identifies the mechanism that would still hold up in a real founder, product, or investor conversation.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by mechanism, not by familiar vocabulary. Pick the answer you would defend in a real decision conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "Whether a repeatable sales process and business model can be validated."

questions.text: Which evidence is strongest before scaling sales?
questions.explanation: Repeatable conversion in a narrow segment is stronger than scattered one-off enthusiasm.

options.text: A. Several similar customers move through the same sales steps for the same core reason.
B. One large prospect says maybe next year.
C. The founder has many LinkedIn likes.
D. The demo gets compliments from friends.

conceptHelp.title: Customer Validation: Which evidence is strongest before scaling sales
conceptHelp.summary: Customer Validation is tested here through a concrete operating question: "Which evidence is strongest before scaling sales?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The strongest answer identifies the mechanism that would still hold up in a real founder, product, or investor conversation.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by mechanism, not by familiar vocabulary. Pick the answer you would defend in a real decision conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "Several similar customers move through the same sales steps for the same core reason."

questions.text: What should happen if customer validation fails?
questions.explanation: Failed validation should trigger a return to discovery or a pivot in customer, problem, product, or go-to-market assumptions.

options.text: A. Scale hiring anyway because the plan was approved.
B. Return to discovery or pivot the weak customer, problem, product, or sales assumption.
C. Hide the failed evidence from the team.
D. Rename the product and keep the same motion.

conceptHelp.title: Customer Validation: What should happen if customer validation fails
conceptHelp.summary: Customer Validation is tested here through a concrete operating question: "What should happen if customer validation fails?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: The strongest answer identifies the mechanism that would still hold up in a real founder, product, or investor conversation.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Compare the options by mechanism, not by familiar vocabulary. Pick the answer you would defend in a real decision conversation.
conceptHelp.example: The strongest answer is: "Return to discovery or pivot the weak customer, problem, product, or sales assumption."
